truth

Kurtz dies after uttering 'The horror! The horror!' Marlow survives, returns to Europe disillusioned, protects parts of Kurtz's legacy, and lies to Kurtz's Intended by telling her that Kurtz's last word was her name.

The ending frames Kurtz's final cry as a terrible moment of self-recognition and leaves Marlow burdened with the knowledge that civilization's ideals often conceal greed, violence, and inner darkness.
When power is unchecked by restraint, the darkness within us takes command.
Fine ideals mean little when greed and self-deception rule the heart.
To face the truth about human nature is painful, but blindness is more dangerous.

Marlow returns to Europe profoundly changed and haunted by Kurtz's memory. He visits Kurtz's grieving fiancée and, unable to shatter her pure but naive faith, lies by telling her that Kurtz's last word was her name.

The resolution highlights the fragile illusions that sustain civilization. Marlow compromises his strict hatred of lies to protect the innocent from the devastating truth of human depravity, suggesting that society functions only by ignoring the darkness at its core.
The veneer of civilization is fragile and easily stripped away to reveal the primal darkness within human nature.
Absolute power and isolation, devoid of societal restraint, inevitably lead to moral corruption and madness.
Society often relies on comforting illusions and lies to shield itself from the horrifying truths of its own existence.

Marlow brings Kurtz back from the inner station, but Kurtz dies after recognizing the horror of his own life. Marlow survives illness, returns to Europe disillusioned, and preserves Kurtz's fiancée's illusion by lying about his final words.

The ending suggests that confronting evil may yield insight but not purity; Marlow gains moral knowledge yet remains compromised, showing how deeply corruption and illusion shape both empire and individual conscience.
